config.prompt: Name the first 10 U.S. Presidents in order

items.explanation:
1st President (1789–1797), Federalist (no formal party)
Commander-in-Chief of the Continental Army during the Revolutionary War
Only president unanimously elected by the Electoral College — twice
Set the two-term precedent that held until FDR
Presided over the Constitutional Convention in Philadelphia, 1787
Farewell Address warned against political parties and foreign entanglements
Died in 1799 at Mount Vernon; at death was the wealthiest president in history (adjusted)

2nd President (1797–1801), Federalist
First president to live in the White House (moved in November 1800)
Defended British soldiers in court after the Boston Massacre (1770)
Signed the Alien and Sedition Acts, which criminalized dissent
First president to be defeated for reelection
Died on July 4, 1826 — the same day as Thomas Jefferson, exactly 50 years after the Declaration
Father of John Quincy Adams (6th President) — first father-son presidential pair

3rd President (1801–1809), Democratic-Republican
Principal author of the Declaration of Independence (1776)
Engineered the Louisiana Purchase (1803), doubling U.S. territory for ~$15 million
Founded the University of Virginia; designed its campus himself
Sent Lewis and Clark on their expedition (1804–1806)
Inventor and polymath: redesigned the plow, built a revolving book stand, played violin
Died July 4, 1826 — same day as John Adams, on the Declaration's 50th anniversary

4th President (1809–1817), Democratic-Republican
'Father of the Constitution' — principal architect and author of the Bill of Rights
Shortest president at 5'4" and never weighed more than ~100 lbs
Led the nation through the War of 1812 against Britain
Only sitting president to personally command troops in battle (Bladensburg, 1814)
The White House was burned by the British during his presidency
His wife Dolley Madison famously saved Gilbert Stuart's portrait of Washington from the fire

5th President (1817–1825), Democratic-Republican
Issued the Monroe Doctrine (1823): the Americas are closed to further European colonization
Presidency known as the 'Era of Good Feelings' — ran virtually unopposed in 1820
Last president who was a Founding Father and Revolutionary War veteran
Wounded at the Battle of Trenton (1776) alongside Washington
Oversaw the Missouri Compromise (1820), temporarily resolving the slavery expansion debate
Died July 4, 1831 — the third president to die on Independence Day

6th President (1825–1829), Democratic-Republican / National Republican
Son of John Adams — first son of a president to become president (until George W. Bush)
Won the presidency via the House of Representatives ('Corrupt Bargain' of 1824)
Only former president to serve in the U.S. House of Representatives (1831–1848)
Fluent in seven languages; served as diplomat in Europe from age 14
Argued the Amistad case before the Supreme Court (1841), winning freedom for enslaved Africans
Collapsed on the House floor and died in the Capitol building, 1848

7th President (1829–1837), Democrat
Founder of the modern Democratic Party; his election called the 'rise of the common man'
Hero of the Battle of New Orleans (1815) — won decisively after the peace treaty was signed
First president to survive an assassination attempt (1835, both pistols misfired)
Destroyed the Second Bank of the United States, sparking the Panic of 1837
Forced the Indian Removal Act (1830), leading to the Trail of Tears
Nicknamed 'Old Hickory' for his toughness; fought in multiple duels, carrying bullets in his body

8th President (1837–1841), Democrat
First president born a U.S. citizen (not a British colonial subject)
First president born speaking a language other than English (Dutch, grew up in Kinderhook, NY)
Nicknamed 'The Little Magician' for his political skill and 'Old Kinderhook' (possibly the origin of 'OK')
Presidency dominated by the Panic of 1837, one of the worst financial crises in U.S. history
Lost reelection to William Henry Harrison in 1840 ('Tippecanoe and Tyler Too')
Ran again in 1848 on the Free Soil ticket, opposing the expansion of slavery

9th President (1841), Whig
Shortest presidency in history — died 31 days after inauguration
Delivered the longest inaugural address (nearly 2 hours, in cold rain without a coat)
Died of pneumonia (or possibly typhoid) on April 4, 1841
Campaigned with the famous slogan 'Tippecanoe and Tyler Too'
'Tippecanoe' referred to his 1811 victory over Tecumseh's confederacy at the Battle of Tippecanoe
Grandfather of Benjamin Harrison (23rd President) — only grandparent-grandchild presidential pair

10th President (1841–1845), Whig (expelled from party)
First VP to assume presidency upon a president's death — set the precedent for succession
Nicknamed 'His Accidency' by opponents who questioned his legitimacy
Expelled from his own party; his entire Cabinet resigned except Daniel Webster
Annexed Texas in 1845, one of the last acts of his presidency
Fathered 15 children — the most of any president
Died in 1862 as an elected Confederate congressman — the only president to join the Confederacy
